title: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: Tricking a server into making HTTP requests to internal resources on behalf of the attacker.
fullExplanation: SSRF occurs when an attacker can control the URL that a server fetches. The server makes requests from its own network context — bypassing firewalls that block external access to internal services. In cloud environments, SSRF can access the metadata service (169.254.169.254) to steal IAM credentials. Capital One breach (2019) used SSRF to steal AWS credentials from EC2 metadata service — 100M records exposed.

attackScenarios.description: http://internal-server:6379/ — probe internal Redis, Elasticsearch, admin panels
file:///etc/passwd — read local files via file:// scheme
Server is trusted internally — access services blocked to external IPs
No response returned — use DNS/HTTP callbacks (Burp Collaborator) to detect

keyFacts: OWASP Top 10 #10 (2021) — new entry reflecting cloud importance
Capital One breach (2019): SSRF → AWS metadata → IAM credentials → S3 data
AWS IMDSv2 requires PUT request first — mitigates simple SSRF
DNS rebinding: domain resolves to external IP first, then internal IP
Blind SSRF detected via out-of-band DNS/HTTP callbacks
gopher:// and dict:// protocols can interact with non-HTTP services

defenses.name: Input Validation
defenses.description: Whitelist allowed domains/IPs — reject private IP ranges

defenses.name: Block Internal IPs
defenses.description: Firewall rules prevent server from reaching 169.254.x.x, 10.x.x.x, 172.16.x.x, 192.168.x.x

defenses.name: IMDSv2 (AWS)
defenses.description: Require session-oriented metadata requests — PUT first, then GET

defenses.name: Disable Unused URL Schemes
defenses.description: Block file://, gopher://, dict:// — allow only http/https

defenses.name: Network Segmentation
defenses.description: Web servers shouldn't have direct access to internal services
